events.text: Patient is a 66-year-old African American male, height 6'0", weight 224 lbs, BMI 30.4, never smoker, history of type 2 diabetes and stage 3 chronic kidney disease, family history of prostate cancer in brother diagnosed at age 60.

At age 58, patient was diagnosed with prostate adenocarcinoma (ICD-O site C61.9, histology 8140/3) after presenting with urinary frequency and a PSA of 28.4 ng/mL; digital rectal exam showed bilateral induration.

At age 58, transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate biopsy showed Gleason 4+5=9 (Grade Group 5) adenocarcinoma involving all 12 cores with extensive perineural invasion and lymphovascular invasion bilaterally.
At age 58, staging bone scan showed increased uptake in the L1 and L4 vertebral bodies and left acetabulum consistent with bone metastases; CT showed a 2.8 cm left obturator lymph node.
At age 58, patient was staged as cT3bN1M1b adenocarcinoma of the prostate with de novo metastatic disease to bone and pelvic lymph nodes.
At age 58, leuprolide 45 mg intramuscular every 6 months was initiated along with abiraterone 1000 mg daily and prednisone 5 mg daily for metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer.
At age 58, after 3 months on combined therapy, PSA declined from 28.4 to 3.6 ng/mL; testosterone was 9 ng/dL.
At age 58, patient developed grade 2 hyperglycemia (HbA1c rose from 7.2% to 9.1%) on prednisone requiring insulin initiation; also grade 1 hypokalemia managed with supplementation.
At age 59, restaging bone scan showed stable uptake at known sites; CT showed decrease in obturator lymph node to 1.2 cm; PSA was 1.8 ng/mL.
At age 60, PSA nadir was 0.8 ng/mL on abiraterone plus ADT; disease remained radiographically stable.

At age 60, next-generation sequencing (Tempus xT) on circulating tumor DNA detected BRCA2 pathogenic mutation (c.6174delT), TP53 splice site mutation, MYC amplification, and AR amplification; tumor fraction was estimated at 8%; microsatellite stable.
At age 62, PSA rose to 6.4 ng/mL on abiraterone with testosterone of 14 ng/dL; castration-resistant progression confirmed.
At age 62, disease reclassified as metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer with rising PSA on abiraterone plus continuous ADT.

At age 62, PSMA PET/CT showed progressive PSMA-avid bone disease with new lesions in bilateral ribs and T6 vertebral body in addition to known lesions; left obturator node had increased to 2.4 cm; no visceral metastatic disease.
At age 62, abiraterone was discontinued and olaparib 300 mg orally twice daily was initiated given BRCA2 pathogenic mutation detected on ctDNA; continued leuprolide.

At age 62, patient developed grade 2 anemia (hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL) and grade 1 elevated creatinine (from 1.6 to 1.9 mg/dL) on olaparib; transfusion of 2 units packed red blood cells was given and olaparib dose was reduced to 250 mg twice daily.
At age 63, PSA declined to 2.2 ng/mL on dose-reduced olaparib; anemia stabilized with hemoglobin 10.1 g/dL.
At age 63, restaging PSMA PET/CT showed decreased uptake at most bone lesions and decrease in obturator lymph node to 1.6 cm; partial response on olaparib.
At age 64, patient developed grade 2 bone pain in the thoracic spine requiring scheduled long-acting morphine 30 mg every 12 hours.
At age 64, palliative radiation therapy 20 Gy in 5 fractions was delivered to the T6 vertebral body for progressive pain despite opioids.
At age 65, PSA rose to 10.8 ng/mL on olaparib with new bone lesions on imaging; olaparib was discontinued after approximately 3 years of therapy.
At age 65, docetaxel 60 mg/m2 intravenously every 3 weeks (dose-reduced for renal impairment) with prednisone 5 mg twice daily was initiated.

At age 65, after cycle 2 of docetaxel, patient developed grade 3 febrile neutropenia (ANC 300/uL, temperature 39.1C) requiring hospitalization, IV antibiotics, and pegfilgrastim; docetaxel dose further reduced to 50 mg/m2.
At age 66, after 6 cycles of dose-reduced docetaxel, PSA was 8.2 ng/mL with stable disease on imaging; treatment break initiated for recovery.

PATHOLOGY REPORT

DIAGNOSIS AND FINDINGS:
At age 58, transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate biopsy showed Gleason 4+5=9 (Grade Group 5) adenocarcinoma involving all 12 cores with extensive perineural invasion and lymphovascular invasion bilaterally.

RADIOLOGY REPORT

FINDINGS:
At age 58, staging bone scan showed increased uptake in the L1 and L4 vertebral bodies and left acetabulum consistent with bone metastases; CT showed a 2.8 cm left obturator lymph node.

Please correlate clinically.

CLINICAL PROGRESS NOTE

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:
At age 58, after 3 months on combined therapy, PSA declined from 28.4 to 3.6 ng/mL; testosterone was 9 ng/dL.

The patient was seen in clinic today for ongoing oncologic management.

scenario_blurb: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC). Rising PSA on ADT. Treated with enzalutamide, abiraterone, docetaxel, or cabazitaxel. Include BRCA1/2 and homologous recombination deficiency testing. Some with bone-predominant and some with visceral metastases.
